author: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy
book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.

The Manichæans Inquire Whence is Evil and by This Question Think They Have Triumphed. Let Them First Know, Which is Most Easy to Do, that Nothing Can Live Without God. Consummate Evil Cannot Be Known Except by the Knowledge of Consummate Good, Which is God.

metadata.author: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy

ccel.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

title: The Manichæans Inquire Whence is Evil and by This Question Think They Have Triumphed. Let Them First Know, Which is Most Easy to Do, that Nothing Can Live Without God. Consummate Evil Cannot Be Known Except by the Knowledge of Consummate Good, Which is God.

source.title: NPNF1-04. Augustine: The Writings Against the Manichaeans and Against the Donatists

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Here perchance some one may say: Whence are sins themselves, and whence is evil in general?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: If from man, whence is man? if from an angel, whence is the angel?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: When it is said, however truly and rightly, that these are from God, it nevertheless seems to those unskillful and possessed of little power to look into recondite matters, that evils and sins are thereby connected, as by a sort of chain, to God.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: By this question they think themselves triumphant, as if forsooth to ask were to know;—would it were so, for in that case no one would be more knowing than myself.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Yet very often in controversy the propounder of a great question, while impersonating the great teacher, is himself more ignorant in the matter concerning which he would frighten his opponent, than he whom he would frighten.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: These therefore suppose that they are superior to the common run, because the former ask questions that the latter cannot answer.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: If therefore when I most unfortunately was associated with them, not in the position in which I have now for some time been, they had raised these objections when I had brought forward this argument, I should have said: I ask that you meanwhile agree with me, which is most easy, that if nothing can shine without God, much less can anything live without God.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Let us not persist in such monstrous opinions as to maintain that any souls whatsoever have life apart from God.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For perchance it may so happen that with me you are ignorant as to this thing, namely whence is evil, let us then learn either simultaneously or in any order, I care not what.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For what if knowledge of the perfection of evil is impossible to man without knowledge of the perfection of good?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For we should not know darkness if we were always in darkness.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But the notion of light does not allow its opposite to be unknown.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But the highest good is that than which there is nothing higher.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But God is good and than Him nothing can be higher.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: God therefore is the highest good.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Let us therefore together so recognize God, and thus what we seek too hastily will not be hidden from us.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: Do you suppose then that the knowledge of God is a matter of small account or desert.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For what other reward is there for us than life eternal, which is to know God?

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For God the Master says: "But this is life eternal, that they might know Thee the only and true God, and Jesus Christ whom thou hast sent."

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: For the soul, although it is immortal, yet because aversion from the knowledge of God is rightly called its death, when it is converted to God, the reward of eternal life to be attained is that knowledge; so that this is, as has been said, eternal life.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: But no one can be converted to God, except he turn himself away from this world.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: This for myself I feel to be arduous and exceedingly difficult, whether it is easy to you, God Himself would have seen.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: I should have been inclined to think it easy to you, had I not been moved by the fact, that, since the world from which we are commanded to turn away is visible, and the apostle says: "The things that are seen are temporal, but the things that are unseen are eternal," you ascribe more importance to the judgment of these eyes than to that of the mind, asserting and believing as you do that there is no shining feather that does not shine from God; and that there are living souls that do not live from God.

verses.book: On Two Souls, Against the Manichæans.
verses.text: These and like things I should either have said to them or considered with myself, for even then, supplicating God with all my bowels, so to speak, and examining as attentively as possible the Scriptures, I should perchance have been able either to say such things or to think them, so far as was necessary for my salvation.
